title: 1. TBox vs ABox (the only schema rule you need)
description: Schema vs data — warehouse rule book vs boxes on shelves.
story:
The warehouse has ONE rule book on the wall. That book lists allowed shelf labels: Product, Symptom, FailureMode, Part. That book is the TBox — it almost never changes.

Trucks deliver boxes: 'washer wm-001 has symptom will-not-drain'. Those boxes are the ABox. A new product is new boxes, never a new rule book.

If you remember only one sentence: TBox = kinds of things; ABox = actual things.
one_liner: TBox = rule book (classes). ABox = boxes (instances). New SKU = new ABox only.
why_it_matters: Stops you inventing a new ontology file per product.
Lets validation check instances against a shared schema.
Interview trap: people say 'ontology' for both — separate them.
say_aloud: TBox is the terminological box: shared classes and relationships.
ABox is the assertional box: facts about specific products.
Adding espresso machine esp-001 is ABox under the same TBox.
TBox changes only when we need a new kind of entity, not a new SKU.

cheat_sheet.meaning: Neo4j holds operational ABox; OWL is export/interchange

beats.title: One triple pattern (memorize)
beats.goal: Every RDF fact is three parts.
beats.narrative: Do not memorize all of rdflib. Memorize: add((subject, predicate, object)).
beats.code:
from rdflib import Graph, Namespace, RDF, OWL, Literal, RDFS

WD = Namespace("https://example.org/wd#")
g = Graph()

# TBox: Product is a class
g.add((WD.Product, RDF.type, OWL.Class))
g.add((WD.Product, RDFS.label, Literal("Product")))

# ABox: this washer is a Product
g.add((WD["wm-001"], RDF.type, WD.Product))
g.add((WD["wm-001"], RDFS.label, Literal("Front Load Washer")))
beats.say_after: Class line uses OWL.Class. Instance line uses WD.Product as the type.

annotations.note: ABox: individual typed as that class.
Human label — not required for logic, useful for people.

line_quiz.why: OWL.Class means vocabulary, not a product instance.
RDF.type WD.Product means this URI is an instance.

pro_tips: TRICK: in Turtle `a` is exactly rdf:type — 'wm-001 a Product' reads like English (W3C RDF Primer §5.1).
GOTCHA: literals (strings/numbers) may only sit in the OBJECT position — never subject or predicate.
TRICK: reuse an existing IRI/vocabulary instead of minting your own — shared IRIs are what let graphs merge.

beats.title: Same idea in Turtle (read-only)
beats.goal: Read three lines of Turtle without panic.
beats.narrative: Turtle is just a compact way to write triples.
beats.say_after: `a` means rdf:type. First line class, second line instance.

concepts.definition: Shared schema: what kinds of nodes/edges are allowed.
concepts.say_aloud: TBox is the rule book on the wall.

concepts.definition: Instance data: this product, this symptom, this edge.
concepts.say_aloud: ABox is the boxes of facts on the shelves.

self_quiz.q: New product SKU — TBox or ABox?

self_quiz.a: Only when you need a new kind of entity/relationship.

common_mistakes: Making one OWL file per product.
Calling Neo4j data 'the ontology' without distinguishing schema vs instances.
final_boss: 45s: warehouse story for TBox vs ABox.
From memory: write 4 rdflib lines — class Product + instance wm-001.
